1:
The chapter turns from the finished temple to Solomon's own great works, and the Puritan and Reformed witnesses seize at once on the arithmetic the verse quietly reports: thirteen years for the king's house against a little more than seven for the house of God. John Gill reads the contrast as a deliberate ordering of loves — Solomon "made more haste with the house of God than with his own," which showed a greater regard to the honour of God than to his own glory or even his convenience. Matthew Henry presses the same point into a maxim: never had any man so much of the spirit of building, and to such purpose, because he "built for God first, and then all his other buildings were comfortable"; the surest foundations of lasting prosperity are those laid in early piety (Matt. 6:33). The difference, Henry insists, was not that Solomon was more exact upon the temple but that he was "less eager and intent" upon his own palace, "in no haste for his own palace, but impatient till the temple was finished and fit for use."

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the plain facts and set the stage for the buildings that follow. Adam Clarke identifies this house as the royal residence in Jerusalem, what some call the king's winter house, called elsewhere simply "the king's house" (1 Kings 9:10), and marvels through the rest of the chapter at the sheer scale of the undertaking. Keil and Delitzsch, opening this section, set the palace-complex within the reign whose glory the books of Kings will trace, and read the following verses as a survey of its parts — the house of the forest of Lebanon, the pillar-hall, the throne-hall, and the private dwellings. Henry lays out the same catalogue, counting a house for the king, the forest house, a porch of pillars, a hall of judgment, and a house for the queen (v.8).

Beneath the survey the company is united in a lesson drawn from the bare comparison of years. The magnificence is real and, Henry allows, not to be grudged — it was no reflection on David that the son should build better than the father, "in proportion to the estate wherewith God had blessed him," and much of the comfort of this life is bound up with an agreeable house. Yet the ordering of the work preaches its own sermon: "we ought to prefer God's honour before our own ease and satisfaction." The verse that opens a chapter of royal splendour thus begins by measuring that splendour against the temple, and finds, in the very slowness of the palace, the proof that Solomon's first care had been for God.
2:
The witnesses take up the first and largest of the royal buildings, the house of the forest of Lebanon, and divide chiefly over its purpose and the reason for its name. On the name they are of one mind, and all reject the natural guess. John Gill insists it was not built on Mount Lebanon, which lay far to the north on the border of the land, but was so called both because it was framed of the cedars of Lebanon and because it stood "in a situation, and among groves of trees which resembled it"; he takes it for a summer house, and cites the Targum's happy phrase, "a royal house of refreshment." Adam Clarke agrees it was not in Lebanon but probably on Mount Zion, and thinks it earned its name simply because it was "built almost entirely of materials brought from that place."

The grammatical-historical reading of Keil and Delitzsch supplies the architecture and the likeliest use. The building was named, "so to speak," from being built of a forest of cedar pillars, and the Arabic calls it "the house of his arms," for according to 1 Kings 10:17 it also served as an armoury. Keil will not reduce it to a mere arsenal, however, but supposes it was meant for other purposes as well. He fixes its scale from the text — "an hundred cubits its length, fifty cubits its breadth, and thirty cubits its height, on four rows of cedar pillars, and hewn cedar beams over the pillars" — and observes that, since it had side-rooms above the pillars (v.3), it can hardly be pictured except as chambers raised upon four rows of columns. Gill likewise gathers from later chapters that it was both a magazine of arms and a court of judicature.

The division among them is one of emphasis rather than fact: was this vast cedar hall a place of pleasure, a treasury of weapons, or a seat of judgment? Gill leans to refreshment, the Arabic tradition to arms, the text itself toward all three, and Keil is content to leave its full use open. What the witnesses agree upon is the impression the measurements make. A hall a hundred cubits long, upheld by rows of cedar like a planted grove, was a work of royal magnificence, and its very name carried the memory of that far mountain whose timber had already gone into the house of God. The palace of the king, like the temple before it, was to be clothed in the cedars of Lebanon.
3:
This verse describes the roofing and upper storeys of the forest-house, and here the grammatical-historical witnesses part company over a genuine puzzle of construction. The difficulty is what the "forty-five pillars, fifteen in a row" belong to. John Gill takes the plain surface sense: on a second floor stood three rows of pillars, fifteen in each, making forty-five, and upon these ran beams that formed the floor of a third storey, roofed over with cedar. Keil and Delitzsch, weighing the Hebrew more closely, argue that the numbers cannot refer to the pillars at all but to the side-rooms (the same word used of the temple's chambers in 1 Kings 6:5). On their reading there were forty-five side-rooms built upon the lower rows of columns, "in ranges of fifteen each," and this could only be, they say, if the rooms were set not side by side but one above another — three storeys of fifteen chambers apiece, exactly as in the side-building of the temple.

Keil is candid that he once held the other view, and lays out why he abandoned it: to make the forty-five mere galleries or pillar-halls above the lower columns is "at variance" with the plain mention of side-rooms, which require enclosed chambers, not open colonnades. The word for roofing he takes in the sense it bears at 1 Kings 6:15, of a cedar covering laid over the whole. Thus the two schemes yield very different buildings: Gill's, a hall of tiered colonnades open to the air; Keil's, a three-storeyed block of chambers resting on a forest of pillars and closed above with cedar.

The dispute is real but bounded, and it is a dispute of reconstruction rather than of doctrine. Both agree on the materials and on the general grandeur; both agree that cedar covered all; both are trying only to picture rightly a structure the writer describes in the compressed idiom of an inventory. It is worth marking how carefully the witnesses labour over so bare a notice — counting pillars, distinguishing rooms from ranges, comparing the temple's chambers — for it is the instinct of the grammatical-historical school to let no measurement pass unexamined, trusting that the inspired historian recorded these numbers because they answer to a real house that once stood, whose very cedar roof and tiered chambers can, with patience, be seen again.
4:
The notice of the windows draws the witnesses into a study of the great hall's light and symmetry. John Gill takes the "three rows" of windows to run along the second and third storeys on the east, north, and south, "there being none in the west, where the porch stood," and reads "light against light in three ranks" to mean that the windows of the tiers were set directly opposite one another. Matthew Henry, surveying these buildings, catches the design behind the detail: the forest-house was "designed for pleasant prospects," and so its windows were ranged "light against light," or, as he prefers to render it, "prospect against prospect" — a hall built to look out upon the trees that gave it its name.

The grammatical-historical care of Keil and Delitzsch presses the harder Hebrew. They render the clause "all the doorways and mouldings were square of beam," and note that the Greek translators used a broader word for a wide space, which some would follow to read of the windows or outlooks rather than the mere doorposts. Keil records the proposed emendations of Thenius without simply adopting them, and observes that the "doors" of the verse were both the inner doors connecting the chambers and the outer doors reached, "of course by stairs, probably winding staircases," through the several storeys. The effect of his patient work is to confirm the picture Gill and Henry draw: an ordered building of squared openings and answering windows, rank over rank.

Where the plainer readers see prospect and the philologist sees the difficulty of the text, all arrive at the same impression of studied regularity. The threefold repetition — three rows of windows, three ranks of light, three storeys behind them — speaks of a symmetry deliberately sought, so that the eye passing along the cedar front met opening answering to opening in perfect correspondence. The witnesses do not moralize the windows, but they leave standing the sense that even in his own house Solomon built by measure and proportion, the same love of order that had governed the courts of the temple now shaping the halls of the king, light set against light and prospect against prospect throughout.
5:
This short verse rounds off the account of the windows and doorways begun in v.4, and the witnesses, with little to add to so plain a carpenter's note, use it to fix the finished look of the forest-house. John Gill reads it at face value: "the doors into the several stories and apartments, and the posts and lintel of them, and the windows over them, were all square" — a building of clean rectangular openings, storey answering to storey, with nothing arched or irregular in the whole design. The word "square" is pressed by all of them as the governing note of the structure, a rectilinear order carried from the ground to the roof.

The grammatical-historical reading of Keil and Delitzsch, carried over from their long note on the previous verse, wrestles with the obscure phrase our version renders "square with the windows." They would read "all the doorways and mouldings were square of beam," taking the material and mode of execution as the point, and they record the ancient variant that turned the doorposts into "outlooks" or broad spaces — a reading which, if right, would make the whole framed opening, and not merely the post, the thing described as squared. Keil is cautious here, rehearsing the proposed emendation of Thenius and the Greek rendering without forcing a decision, content to establish that the openings were of a squared, four-cornered form and that "light was against light in three ranks," window facing window through the three storeys.

So slight a verse yields no doctrine, and the witnesses attempt none; yet it is not idle. The repeated insistence on the square and the ranked windows completes the impression the earlier verses began, of a palace whose beauty lay in exact proportion rather than ornament for its own sake. The forest-house, like the temple it followed, was built to rule and measure, every door and lintel answering to the next, so that even a bare inventory of joinery testifies to the ordered magnificence of Solomon's work. The historian lingers over the carpentry, and the commentators after him, because the greatness of these buildings lay as much in their disciplined symmetry as in the costliness of their cedar and stone.
6:
The witnesses turn to the porch, or hall, of pillars, and the chief interest is its place and function within the palace. John Gill sets it plainly "at the west end of the house," while Charles Ellicott makes the most careful case for its purpose. Though some hold it a separate building, its dimensions betray it: its length of fifty cubits is exactly the breadth of the great hall, so that it seems to have been "a propylæon, or entrance vestibule, to the hall of state," answering to the porch or vestibule of the temple, and probably matching it in the arrangement of its pillars and perhaps in height. Ellicott presses the last clause of the verse to a better rendering — "a porch before it with pillars, and a threshold before them" — picturing a colonnaded approach with a raised plinth or, possibly, a flight of steps.

The grammatical-historical reconstruction of Keil and Delitzsch agrees on the essentials and adds a judgment about its position. Because the pillar-hall is named between the house of the forest of Lebanon and the throne-hall, Keil infers that it stood between them, a hall fifty cubits by thirty, with a further porch "in front of them." Since its length equals the breadth of the forest-house, he supposes it ran parallel to that building, "fifty cubits broad and thirty cubits deep." Matthew Henry gives the vestibule a homelier use: he takes these "piazzas" or porch of pillars to have served, it may be, "for an exchange or a guard-house," or as a covered place for those who attended the king on business "to walk in... till they could have audience," or simply for state and magnificence.

The company differs only in stress — Ellicott and Keil reconstructing the vestibule as the architects of the record, Henry imagining the crowd of suitors pacing under its columns — but they converge on a single picture: a grand colonnaded entrance leading the visitor toward the seat of the king. Henry cannot resist drawing the pillared hall toward Wisdom's house, which "hath hewn out her seven pillars" (Prov. 9:1) for the shelter of those who wait daily at her gates. The porch of pillars, on every reading, is the threshold of majesty, a place of waiting and approach where the splendour of cedar and stone prepared the mind for the throne to which it led.
7:
Beyond the pillared vestibule stood the porch of the throne, and the witnesses read it as the heart of Solomon's public office: the hall where the king judged. John Gill notes at once that here was set "the ivory throne on which he sat to hear and try causes," pointing forward to its famous description (1 Kings 10:18-20). Adam Clarke agrees that this porch "appears to have been devoted to the purposes of administering judgment, which Solomon did in person." The verse thus fixes in stone the wisdom for which the young king had asked and been so richly granted, giving his justice a visible and permanent seat.

The grammatical-historical witnesses debate its structure and its relation to the pillar-hall. Keil and Delitzsch hold that the throne-hall and the judgment-hall are one and the same, "both a court of judgment and an audience-chamber," in which stood the splendid throne of the tenth chapter. They wrestle with the obscure phrase "from floor to floor" (or, as some read, from one wall to the other), which describes how the cedar covering ran, and note that this hall followed immediately upon the pillar-hall yet was clearly distinguished from it by the repetition of the verb "he made." Charles Ellicott takes the judgment-hall to be a separate building, floored and ceiled with cedar, and reports Fergusson's conjecture, drawn from Assyrian and Persian remains, that it was square, "supported on four pillars in the centre, between which the throne stood," with openings on the four sides for the public, the king, and his officers.

The difference — whether the throne-hall stood distinct or ran on from the colonnade — is a matter of reconstruction, and beneath it the witnesses are agreed on the meaning. This was the place where the king made himself accessible, where causes were "adjusted in the king's presence" and the humblest suit could reach the throne. The cedar-covered hall, whatever its exact shape, framed an act rather than an ornament: Solomon judging his people. In building so grand a seat for justice, the witnesses observe, Solomon confessed that the first business of a king is not splendour but righteousness, and set the throne of judgment, richly wainscoted from floor to roof, at the very approach to his house.
8:
Behind the throne-hall lay the private quarters — the king's own dwelling and a house built for Pharaoh's daughter — and the witnesses reconstruct their arrangement while marking the shadow that falls across the second of them. Keil and Delitzsch place the royal palace immediately behind the throne and judgment hall, its principal entrance probably through that hall, "so that the king really delivered judgment and granted audiences in the gate of his palace." His dwelling stood "in the other court inwards from the hall," built like the throne-hall, and formed, when one looked from the entrance, the hindmost court of the whole complex; for the queen a separate house was raised, "like unto this porch." Adam Clarke counts three houses in all — the king's palace, the house of judgment, and this third, "the harem, or apartments for the women" — probably joined in one building yet distinguished in use.

The grammatical-historical reading leaves the exact construction uncertain, since the hall to which the dwellings are compared is nowhere fully described; all that can be clearly gathered, Keil allows, is that the king and his Egyptian wife had a court of their own, set apart at the rear. Gill and the others attend to the plain sense — the place where the king "usually resided," and a fit house for the daughter of the king of Egypt whom Solomon had taken to wife.

It is Matthew Henry who hears the warning in the arrangement. The queen's house, he observes, "was nearer to his own palace"; and then, with a sigh, "yet perhaps if it had been as near as it ought to have been Solomon would not have multiplied wives as he did." The mention of Pharaoh's daughter, honourable in itself, is for Henry the first small crack in a house otherwise so wisely built, a foreign marriage set in cedar and stone that would prove, in the sad sequel of the reign (1 Kings 11:1), the beginning of Solomon's undoing. The witnesses do not press the point here, but Henry will not let the reader admire the private courts of the palace without remembering whose apartments they were, and where such alliances would lead.
9:
With the several buildings surveyed, the historian turns to the quality of the stone-work, and the witnesses dwell on its costliness and its finish. John Gill glosses the "costly stones" as marble, porphyry, and the like — the best of their kind. Keil and Delitzsch unfold the compressed clause with characteristic care: all these buildings were of "costly stones, after the measure of that which is hewn, sawn with the saw within and without" — that is, dressed on both the inner and outer faces of the halls — "and from the foundation to the corbels, and from without to the great court." They labour over the word rendered "coping," which the Greek took for the projecting cornice and Thenius for the roof's stone border, and they distinguish the "outside" from the great court as the outer court of the palace, so that the fine masonry ran uniformly through the whole enclosure.

The point the witnesses draw from this is the thoroughness of the workmanship. These were not rough field-stones faced only where they showed; they were sawn smooth on every side, "within and without," from the deepest foundation to the highest corbel. Matthew Henry, gathering up the description that runs through these verses, marks it as the governing principle of all Solomon's building: "all the materials were the best of their kind," the foundation-stones costly for their size and the wrought stones costly for their labour, "hewn and sawn, and in all respects finely wrought." Nothing was slighted because it lay hidden; the parts unseen were finished as carefully as the parts in view.

Here the grammatical-historical eye for detail and the Puritan eye for edification meet. What the one records as the marks of the saw on every face, the other reads as an image of integrity — a work honest to its very foundations, sound where no eye but God's would ever look. The costly stones, sawn within and without, testify that Solomon spared no pains upon the substructure of his greatness; and Henry, without straining the figure, leaves the reader to feel that a life, like a palace, is well built only when its hidden courses are as true as its front. The magnificence the following verses will number begins here, in stone dressed faithfully on the side that no one sees.
10:
The witnesses fasten on the sheer magnitude of the foundation-stones, which the text measures in cubits. John Gill notes simply that they were "of a great price, and very large," great stones laid to bear great buildings. Keil and Delitzsch give the fuller reckoning: "the foundation was laid with costly, large stones of ten and eight cubits" in length, "and of corresponding breadth and thickness," and above this footing the visible walls rose in "costly stones, after the measure of that which is hewn, and cedars." The foundation was thus a course of hewn blocks some twelve to fifteen feet long, a base of proportioned to the mass it upheld.

Keil carries the description into the surrounding wall, for the verse leads on to the great court, "formed of three rows of hewn stones and a row of hewn cedar beams, as in the inner court of the house of Jehovah" (compare 1 Kings 6:36). He notes the small grammatical turn by which the writer likens the palace court to the temple court, and defends the received text against needless conjecture, comparing the idiom to the usage of the Proverbs (Prov. 25:20). The measured stones and the ordered courses are, to the grammatical-historical reader, evidence of a real and reconstructable building, its proportions preserved in the very numbers the historian took care to record.

The devotional witnesses draw the lesson these vast stones invite. Matthew Henry observes that "the foundation-stones were costly for their size," four or five yards square or long, and the building-stones "costly for the workmanship," and takes the whole as an instance of the care that spares nothing for a lasting work. That the palace court was made "like that of the temple" pleases him especially: Solomon "so well did he like the model of God's courts that he made his own by it." Here again the meanest part of the structure, the buried footing on which everything rested, is dwelt upon rather than passed over, for the witnesses agree that greatness endures only where its foundations are laid deep and true. The costly stones beneath the ground, like the piety of an early life, are what hold the visible splendour up.
11:
This verse continues the account of the masonry, and the witnesses read it as of one piece with those before. John Gill takes "above were costly stones" to mean that from the foundation upward, "from thence to the top of the buildings, the whole walls were made of such right up to the ceiling" — the same costly, hewn stone that formed the footing carried all the way to the roof. There was no cheapening of the fabric as it rose out of sight of the eye; the wall was of a piece from base to coping.

The grammatical-historical reading of Keil and Delitzsch gathers this verse with the next, describing how "the great court round about was with three rows of hewed stones, and a row of cedar beams, both for the inner court of the house of the LORD, and for the porch of the house." Keil defends the received text against the emendation of Thenius, explaining the connecting particle as a mark of comparison — "and so with the court" — and citing the parallel idiom in the Proverbs (Prov. 25:20). He takes the "porch of the house" here to refer not to the temple-hall but to the pillar-hall of the palace, whose surrounding wall was built in the same fashion as the wall of the great court. The whole enclosure, on his reading, answered in its courses to the inner court of the temple (1 Kings 6:36).

The verse yields no debate of substance, only the patient labour of reconstruction and the quiet moral it carries by repetition. Three times over — foundation, wall, and court — the historian insists upon costly, hewn stone, and the witnesses insist with him, refusing to hurry past the masonry. What emerges is a picture of thoroughgoing solidity: a palace whose every course, seen and unseen, was of the same enduring material as the courts of God's house. The likeness to the temple, drawn out by Keil and dwelt on by the devotional readers, is the point the chapter keeps making in stone — that Solomon, having built for God by rule and measure, would build for himself by the same pattern, so that even the walls of his own house should echo the sanctuary.
12:
The account of the palace closes with its great enclosing court, and the witnesses read it as the frame that gathered the whole complex together. John Gill takes "the great court round about" to be the wall "which surrounded Solomon's house," the outer boundary within which the several buildings stood. Charles Ellicott fills out the picture: this great court "is said to have resembled the inner court of the Temple," having "an enclosure of three rows of stones, probably of large size, with a cedar coping." It plainly enclosed the whole palace, and Ellicott supposes it "may have contained quarters for the guards and the household," with inner courts besides, around which the more public and the more private buildings were grouped.

The grammatical-historical interest here is the explicit likeness the text draws to the sanctuary. The palace court was made "both for the inner court of the house of the LORD, and for the porch of the house" — that is, after the same manner as the temple's inner court (1 Kings 6:36), three courses of hewn stone crowned with a row of cedar. Keil and Delitzsch, as noted on the previous verses, take the comparison to govern the construction of the whole enclosure, so that the reader is meant to see the king's court and the Lord's court built to one design.

That deliberate correspondence is what the witnesses finally draw out. The historian did not merely record the palace's outer wall; he measured it against the house of God, and the commentators hear in that measurement a confession of order and reverence. Solomon "so well did he like the model of God's courts that he made his own by it," in Matthew Henry's phrase — the pattern of the sanctuary shaping even the boundary of the royal grounds. The chapter thus ends its survey of the palace where it began, with the temple in view: the king's greatness enclosed within a wall like God's, three rows of stone and a course of cedar, so that no one might approach the seat of majesty without being reminded of the greater house whose model it copied. From this enclosure the narrative now leaves stone and cedar behind and turns to the brass and gold of the sanctuary itself.
13:
The narrative leaves the palace and returns to the house of God, and the witnesses begin with the man Solomon summoned to work its metal: Hiram of Tyre. All are careful to distinguish him from the king of the same name. John Gill notes that this was "not the king of Tyre, but an artificer in it," sent to Solomon at his request, whom Clement of Alexandria calls Hyperon. Adam Clarke describes him as "a very intelligent coppersmith, of Jewish extraction by his mother's side," probably married to a Tyrian. Charles Ellicott observes that the fuller record in the Chronicles (2 Chronicles 2:13-14) shows Solomon asking the Tyrian king for "a man cunning to work," and receiving in answer this master craftsman, whose mixed parentage would let him "enter into the spirit of the Israelite worship, and yet bring to bear upon it the practical skill of the Tyrian artificer."

The grammatical-historical witnesses treat the verse as a supplementary note, and Keil and Delitzsch render its verb as a pluperfect — Solomon "had sent and fetched" Hiram — explaining the honorific title given him in the Chronicles, where his name carries the addition "abi," a word of honour equivalent to master or counsellor, as in Genesis 45:8. They begin here to unfold the discrepancy over his mother's tribe that the next verse will press.

It is Matthew Henry who draws the lesson the union of nations in one workman invites. Hiram had, "the head of a Tyrian and the heart of an Israelite" — the ingenuity of Tyre joined to the affection of a son of Israel for the house of God — and it was happy that the blood of the two peoples mingled in him, "for thereby he was qualified for the work to which he was designed." As the tabernacle had been built with the wealth of Egypt, Henry observes, so the temple was adorned with "the wit of Tyre," for "God will serve himself by the common gifts of the children of men." The best brass was chosen, "good brass... that which was strongest and looked finest," since God, who is the best, must be honoured with the best. So the great work of casting begins under a Gentile hand consecrated to Israel's God, the skill of the nations pressed into the service of the sanctuary.
14:
The witnesses linger over the workman's parentage and his gifts, and they must first reconcile an old crux: this verse makes Hiram "a widow's son of the tribe of Naphtali," while the Chronicles call his mother "of the daughters of Dan" (2 Chronicles 2:14). John Gill canvasses the several harmonizations offered by the Jewish commentators: she may have belonged to the city of Dan, which lay within the territory of Naphtali; or her own mother may have been a Danite, so that she was "of the daughters of Dan" while her son was reckoned of Naphtali; or she was a Danite married into Naphtali. And if a real error lay in it, Gill adds, it must be laid not to the sacred historian but to the king of Tyre, whose words the Chronicler reports, and who might well be uncertain of an Israelite's tribe. Adam Clarke proposes the neatest solution — that she was "of Naphtali by her father, and of Dan by her mother, and so be indifferently called of the tribe of Naphtali or of the daughters of Dan."

Beyond the genealogy, the witnesses hear in the description of Hiram a deliberate echo. He was "filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning to work all works in brass" — the very language once used of Bezalel and Oholiab, the Spirit-filled artificers of the tabernacle (compare Exodus 31:3). Keil and Delitzsch take the verse in stride and move to the pillars, noting that Hiram "came to king Solomon, and wrought all his work," and that the Chronicles reckon the two pillars together at thirty-five cubits (2 Chronicles 3:15), a figure the next verses will require them to explain.

What the company draws from the verse is the dignity conferred on skilled labour in the service of God. The endowment of the craftsman is named as a fullness of wisdom, as though the ability to cast and carve brass were itself a gift of the same Spirit that inspired prophets, so that the artisan takes his place beside the priest and the singer in the work of the house. The one who came "filled with wisdom" to work in metal is honoured by the historian precisely as a wise man, and the witnesses, untroubled by the discrepancy of tribes, are content to see in Hiram a second Bezalel, raised up and equipped for the adorning of a second and greater sanctuary.
15:
The first great works of brass are the two pillars, and the witnesses reckon their dimensions and confront the numbers the parallel records give. The verse makes each pillar eighteen cubits high, with a line of twelve cubits compassing it about. Adam Clarke renders this into English measure — "about thirty feet" high and twelve cubits in circumference — and pauses in frank admiration: "it would be difficult even now to procure a founder who could cast such massive pillars, whether solid or hollow." The scale of the casting, to Clarke the practical man, is itself a wonder, a feat of ancient metal-work not easily matched in his own age.

The chief labour of the witnesses is to square the eighteen cubits here with the thirty-five cubits assigned to the pillars in the Chronicles (2 Chronicles 3:15). John Gill offers two ways of harmonizing. The thirty-five, he suggests, may be "the length or height of them both" taken together; and since two pillars of eighteen would come to thirty-six, not thirty-five, either the round number is used, or "half a cubit in each may be allowed" for the base or pedestal into which the pillar was set, or for the chapiter into which its top ran, "so only what was seen is described." Keil and Delitzsch read the statement of one pillar's height and the other's circumference as an abbreviated way of saying that both were alike in height and thickness, and they too grapple with the Chronicler's larger figure.

The difference is not a contradiction the witnesses fear but a measurement they set themselves to reconcile, and their patience is instructive. Rather than pit one inspired book against another, Gill weighs whether the two accounts measure the pillars singly or together, and whether the buried and hidden portions are counted in or left out. Beneath the arithmetic all agree on the essential fact: two colossal columns of brass, some thirty feet high and vast in girth, cast whole by Hiram's art, were raised at the entrance of the house of God. The witnesses number their cubits so carefully because they believe the historian numbered them for a reason, and because the very magnitude of the pillars — too great, Clarke thought, for a later founder to attempt — declared the glory of the sanctuary they adorned.
16:
Upon the pillars Hiram set two chapiters, or capitals, of molten brass, and the witnesses describe their form and again wrestle with a discrepant number. John Gill pictures the capitals as "large ovals in the form of a crown," as the Hebrew word suggests, "or like two crowns joined together," or, as they are elsewhere called, bowls — a swelling, crown-like head crowning each shaft. Keil and Delitzsch give the measure: each capital was "five cubits the height of the one and of the other," cast in brass to sit upon the tops of the pillars.

The crux is that a later passage assigns the capital a height of only three cubits (2 Kings 25:17). Keil and Delitzsch refuse the easy expedient of supposing the capitals had been reduced by two cubits over time, and judge instead that the discrepancy "rests, like the parallel passage in Jeremiah (Jeremiah 52:22), upon an error of the text" — the confusion of the Hebrew letter for three with the letter for five, whose shapes closely resemble one another. It is a characteristic instance of the grammatical-historical method: where numbers clash, the scholars look first to the well-known slips of the copyist's pen rather than to any fault in the original record.

The witnesses do not moralize the capitals, but they take care to see them rightly, for these crowning ovals were the most conspicuous ornament of the two great pillars. Their crown-like shape, Gill's "two crowns joined together," lent the columns the look of royal standards set before the door, and the following verses will load them with nets, wreaths, pomegranates, and lily-work until the head of each pillar was a garden of wrought brass. Here the interest is simply the plain fact, five cubits of moulded metal, and the honest reckoning of the harder text. The company is agreed that the true height was five cubits, that the three of the later chapter is a scribal error, and that the capitals, whatever the arithmetic, were made to crown the pillars with a fitting magnificence. It is worth marking, too, how the witnesses treat such a clash of numbers without alarm: rather than set 2 Kings 25:17 against this verse as a flat contradiction, they trace the difference to the ordinary hazards of transmission, the near-identical letters that a wearied scribe might exchange, and so preserve at once their confidence in the record and their honesty before its difficulties.
17:
The chapiters were clothed with ornament, and the witnesses attend to the net-work and chain-work that covered them. John Gill distinguishes the two: the "nets of checker work" were like "thick branches of trees, with their boughs and leaves curiously wrought," as the word implies, while the "wreaths of chain work" resembled fringes, "such as the Jews wore at the skirt of their garments." Keil and Delitzsch read the ornament as "seven twists arranged as festoons," plaited cords hung round the capitals like garlands, seven upon each. The head of each pillar was thus wrapped in a lattice of interwoven brass, a decoration at once of foliage and of chain.

Here Charles Ellicott raises a text-critical caution against the received reading. The "seven for the one chapiter, and seven for the other," he judges, "is probably an erroneous reading"; it should be "a net-work for the one chapiter, and a net-work for the other." On his view the number seven has crept in by corruption, and the sense is simply that each capital had its own lattice-work covering, without any count of seven festoons. Keil, by contrast, keeps the seven and makes it the number of the plaited garlands, so that the two scholars of the same school divide over whether "seven" belongs to the text at all.

The disagreement is small and technical, but it shows the witnesses at their proper work — testing the very words of the description, weighing whether a number is original or intrusive, before they will build a picture upon it. What none of them doubts is the lavish artistry of the whole. Whether garlanded sevenfold or simply latticed, the capitals were a wrought thicket of brass, branch and chain woven together, made to catch the eye of everyone who passed between the pillars into the courts of the Lord. The witnesses linger over checker-work and fringe not from antiquarian idleness but because the historian himself dwelt upon them, and they will not pass over any part of the beauty with which the house of God was crowned. The pomegranates that complete the ornament wait for the verses that follow.
18:
This verse adds the pomegranates to the ornament of the chapiters, and the witnesses find in it a knot of textual confusion that they must first untie. Keil and Delitzsch observe that in the Masoretic text "the words for pillars and pomegranates are confused together," so that the verse as it stands has Hiram making "the pillars" where the sense plainly requires "the pomegranates." Following certain manuscripts, they correct the reading in two places, and the meaning becomes clear: "he made pomegranates, and indeed two rows round about the one twist, to cover the capitals... and so he did with the other capital." The emendation, they insist, is not only demanded by the sense but confirmed by comparison with v.19.

With the text set right, the arrangement can be pictured. The relation between the two rows of pomegranates and the plaited net-work is not spelled out, but Keil takes it, with the general judgment of interpreters, that "one row ran round the pillars below the plaited work and the other above," so that the festoons of chain-work were enclosed, top and bottom, by rows of the fruit. John Gill reads the verse in the same direction, taking Hiram to have "adorned" the pillars in this manner, the pomegranates set in ranks about the capitals.

The verse yields no doctrine, and the witnesses attempt none; their labour is entirely to recover, from a corrupted line, the true shape of the ornament. Yet the very trouble they take is itself a kind of reverence. Rather than pass over a confused text or force a meaning upon it, they compare manuscript with manuscript and verse with verse until the description stands coherent again, trusting that the historian left an intelligible account of a real and glorious thing. And the thing, once recovered, is beautiful: the head of each brazen pillar bound about with woven chains and hedged above and below with rows of pomegranates, the fruit of the promised land wrought in metal and lifted up at the door of the house, so that Israel entering the temple passed beneath a harvest of brass. The rows of pomegranates will be numbered again in the summary (v.42), where their full count of four hundred is given.
19:
The witnesses turn to a second decoration of the capitals, the lily-work that crowned their upper part. John Gill describes it as work wrought "in the form of the flower of lilies open," a sculptured blossom spreading at the top of each pillar. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse to show that the chain-work and pomegranates did not cover the whole capital but only its lower portion, while the upper part rose in "lily-work, i.e., sculpture in the form of flowering lilies," set like a crown upon the head of the pillar. Thus the capital had two zones of ornament: below, the latticed festoons hedged with pomegranates; above, the open lily lifting four cubits high.

The obscure clause "four cubits in the porch" (or "after the manner of the hall") gives the grammatical-historical witnesses their difficulty. Keil records the proposal of Böttcher and Thenius that the phrase fixes the pillars' position within the hall, so that their capitals sustained the lintel of the doorway, but he weighs the reading warily, unwilling to build the disputed question of the pillars' place upon so uncertain a word. The four cubits he takes for the height of the lily-crown that occupied the top of the capital.

Beneath the philology the picture is plain and lovely, and the witnesses are content to let it stand. The two mighty columns of brass were crowned not with a bare boss but with a blossoming lily, the same flower whose form had adorned the brim of the molten sea and the ornament of the temple within. The lily-work speaks, as the pomegranates do, of the garden and the field brought into the sanctuary in wrought metal — life and beauty fixed in brass at the threshold of the house of God. The witnesses do not press a meaning upon the flower here, but they mark its prominence, five cubits of capital rising from lattice and fruit to a spreading lily four cubits tall, so that the head of each pillar was a stem in bloom. That the same lily had shaped the brim of the molten sea (v.26) and the carving of the temple walls only strengthens the impression that a single language of flower and fruit ran through the whole house, binding pillar, wall, and laver into one adorned sanctuary. The completion of this lily-work is noted again at v.22.
20:
This verse gathers the ornaments of the capitals into their finished order and numbers the pomegranates, and the witnesses labour over both its obscurity and its arithmetic. The pomegranates stood "above, over against the belly which was by the network," and Charles Ellicott explains the strange word: the "belly," like the "bowls" or globes named later (vv.41-42), signifies "the rounded form of the capital, where it comes down to join the shaft," and it was at this swelling junction that the bands of pomegranate ornament ran round. John Gill reports Dr. Lightfoot's simpler reading, that the pomegranates filled up "the belly of the chapiters, or the middle cubit of them," between the lily-work and the lower ornament.

On the number, Ellicott marks a plain omission in the text. As it stands the verse gives two hundred pomegranates "in rows round about" one capital; but it should read, he argues, "two hundred in rows round about the one chapiter, and two hundred in rows round about the other chapiter," which yields the four hundred of the summary (v.42) and of the Chronicles (2 Chronicles 4:13). The received line has lost, by a copyist's eye, the matching clause for the second pillar.

Here Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the description into the setting-up of the pillars, take a firm stand on the long-disputed question of their place. The words that follow — that Hiram set them up "at the hall of the Holy Place," the Chronicles adding "before the house" (2 Chronicles 3:15-17) — imply, Keil holds, "that the two brazen pillars stood unconnected in front of the hall, on the right and left sides of it, and not within the hall as supporters of the roof." He knows that many have decided otherwise, but he finds no force in the arguments of Thenius for making the columns bear the roof. The verse, then, does two kinds of work at once: it completes the tally of the ornament, four hundred pomegranates in all, and it prepares the reader to see the pillars standing free before the porch — not props of the building, but signs set beside its door, whose meaning the naming in v.21 will declare.
21:
The pillars are raised and named, and here the witnesses pass from measurement to meaning. John Gill places the setting-up not at the very door of the temple but "at the entrance into the porch," where the two columns flanked the way in. Adam Clarke gives the sense of the names: the right pillar, Jachin, "that is, He shall establish"; the left, Boaz, "that is, in strength." And Clarke draws the conclusion that decides the old architectural dispute: "notwithstanding their names, they seem to have supported no part of the building" — they were emblematical, not structural, signs rather than props.

On this the grammatical-historical witnesses are agreed. Keil and Delitzsch, as at the preceding verse, hold that the pillars stood free before the porch, "not within the hall as supporters of the roof," so that their office was wholly significant. The two great columns of brass carried nothing but their names, and it is in the names that the witnesses find the whole point of them.

It is Matthew Henry who unfolds their significancy most fully. Some, he notes, thought them memorials of the pillar of cloud and fire that led Israel through the wilderness; but he rather takes them for "memorandums to the priests and others that came to worship at God's door." Jachin — "he will establish" — bids the worshipper depend upon God only for the fixing of a roving, unstable heart: "it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace." Boaz — "in him is strength" — points the weak away from any sufficiency of their own to the God "who works in us both to will and to do." The pillars were also, Henry adds, a pledge of the stability of the temple and its privileges so long as Israel kept close to God; and he marks the sad sequel, that when the temple was at last destroyed, particular notice was taken of the breaking of these very pillars (2 Kings 25:13), the tokens of an establishment forfeited by apostasy. The witnesses thus turn the two brazen columns, which held up nothing, into the strongest of sermons: at the door of the house stood Establishment and Strength, telling everyone who entered that both were to be sought not in themselves but in God.
22:
This brief verse closes the account of the pillars by repeating that lily-work crowned their tops and declaring the work of the pillars finished. The witnesses take the repetition itself as their subject. John Gill notes that the statement "seems to be repeated from" the earlier notice (v.19) and serves to confirm it. Keil and Delitzsch account for the seeming redundancy by "the significance of this emblem of the capitals of the pillars" — the lily was named twice because it mattered, the crowning ornament worth a second mention — and then the closing words, "so was the work of the capitals finished," bring the long description of this ornament to its end.

The verse is a colophon, a workman's full stop, and the witnesses do not force more from it than it holds. Yet they mark what the repetition accomplishes. By returning to the lily after the nets, the wreaths, and the pomegranates, the historian leaves the eye resting where the pillar itself ended, on the open blossom at its summit; the last word on the capitals is not chain or fruit but flower. The account has moved upward from the shaft through its swelling belly and latticed festoons to close in bloom, so that the finished pillar is remembered as a living stem crowned with lilies.

Something of the whole spirit of these verses gathers in that closing note of completion. Hiram, filled with wisdom, had brought his great work to its appointed end; every part was wrought, numbered, and set in place, and the writer signs it off with the satisfaction of a task done. The pillars, whose names the witnesses have read as Establishment and Strength, now stand finished before the porch, crowned with lily-work, a wrought garden of brass at the door of the house. The repetition that a careless reader might pass over as mere reiteration is, on the witnesses' patient reading, the deliberate sealing of a masterpiece: the work of the pillars was finished, and the historian would have us pause upon it before the narrative moves on to the molten sea. From the door of the house the account now turns to the great laver that stood within the court.
23:
The narrative turns from the pillars to the molten sea, the vast brazen basin of the temple court, and the witnesses describe its dimensions and its use. John Gill explains the name: it was called a sea "because of the great quantity of water it held," after the Hebrew manner of naming any large collection of water a sea. It answered to the brazen laver of the tabernacle, but far larger, and served the priests not only to wash their hands and feet but, the Jewish authorities say, as "a dipping place for the priests." Keil and Delitzsch give the measures — ten cubits across "from the one upper rim to the other," five cubits high, and thirty cubits in circumference — and note with interest that the round numbers of diameter and circumference "come very near to the mathematical proportions," the true ratio being as seven to twenty-two.

It is Matthew Henry, surveying the brass-work, who draws out the significance of so great a vessel. The molten sea was "above five yards in diameter," holding, by his reckoning, above five hundred barrels of water for the priests' washing and the cleansing of the sacrifices and the courts. And he sets it against the tabernacle deliberately: "in the court of the tabernacle there was only a laver of brass provided to wash in, but in the court of the temple a sea of brass, intimating that by the gospel of Christ much fuller preparation is made for our cleansing than was by the law of Moses." Where Moses had a laver, the temple had a sea; and the sea, Henry adds, points forward to "a fountain opened" for sin and uncleanness (Zechariah 13:1).

Here the grammatical-historical eye for proportion and the Puritan eye for type converge on the same object without collision. Keil measures the basin and admires its near-perfect geometry; Henry measures it against the tabernacle and finds in its very greatness a promise of the abundance of grace. Both are reading the same thirty cubits of brimming brass. The molten sea, the witnesses agree, was no mere cistern but a standing testimony that those who draw near to God must be clean, and that God provides, ever more richly under the gospel than under the law, the water in which they may be washed. The oxen that bore it, and the ornament of its brim, the following verses will describe.
24:
The molten sea was ornamented under its brim with a double row of carved figures, and the witnesses divide over what exactly was wrought there. The difficulty arises because this verse speaks of "knops," while the parallel in the Chronicles has the likeness of oxen (2 Chronicles 4:3). John Gill reports the range of opinion: the knops were "of an oval form, and therefore the Targum calls them figures of eggs"; or they were gourds; or, harmonizing with the Chronicles, they bore "the figures of the heads of oxen" and "might serve as cocks to let out the water." Keil and Delitzsch render the word as colocynths, wild gourds, "ten to the cubit, surrounding the sea in two rows," and cast in one piece with the vessel itself.

On the discrepancy with the Chronicles, Keil takes the firm text-critical line his school prefers. He judges the "oxen" of the parallel passage "an evident error of the pen," the Hebrew word for gourds having been miswritten as the word for oxen and then glossed as "figures of oxen." The older harmonization, that the ornaments were "casts of bullocks' heads," he sets aside on the plain ground that the word in question "signifies oxen and not the heads of oxen." Thus where Gill is content to hold the readings together, supposing ox-headed gourds that doubled as spouts, Keil resolves the clash by correcting the parallel and reading simple gourds throughout.

The disagreement is small but characteristic. It turns on whether an apparent difference between two inspired books is to be reconciled by ingenuity, finding a form that satisfies both words, or by textual judgment, tracing one word to a scribe's slip. Gill leans to the first, Keil to the second, and the witnesses leave both paths open to the reader. What none of them doubts is the thing itself: beneath the brimming rim of the great sea ran two close bands of carved fruit, ten to the cubit, wrought in the same casting as the basin, a border of gourds encircling the water like a garland. Even the utensils of washing were made beautiful, the witnesses observe, so that nothing in the house of God, not even a water-vessel, was left plain; and the ornament of gourds beneath the brim answered to the lily-work above it (v.26), fruit below and flower above about the one great sea.
25:
The molten sea did not rest upon the ground but stood lifted upon twelve brazen oxen, and the witnesses attend both to their arrangement and to the meaning some have found in them. Keil and Delitzsch describe the ordered symmetry: the sea rested upon twelve oxen, "three turning to the north, three to the west, three to the south, and three to the east," their hinder parts inward, so that three heads faced each quarter of the heavens. Their size, though the text does not give it, must have been proportioned to the great basin they bore, perhaps some five cubits high to the back, and they stood, Keil supposes, upon a metal plate that fixed them in place. John Gill notes simply that they were "figures of them in brass, of full proportion."

It is Matthew Henry who reports the conjecture that gives the oxen a polemical point. Some think Solomon set images of oxen to bear the sea "in contempt of the golden calf which Israel had worshipped," that, as Bishop Patrick expresses it, "the people might see there was nothing worthy of adoration in those figures; they were fitter to make posts of than to make gods of." An ox of brass set to carry water was a standing rebuke to the ox of gold set up to be adored. Yet Henry adds the sober historical irony: this "prevailed not to prevent Jerusalem's setting up the calves for deities" — the lesson in brass did not keep a later Israel from the very idolatry it reproved.

Here the grammatical-historical care for arrangement and the Puritan ear for admonition meet over the same twelve figures. Keil sees a deliberate cosmic symmetry, the oxen facing outward to the four winds as if the whole earth upheld the vessel of cleansing; Henry sees a quiet sermon against idolatry, the beast that Israel had deified reduced to a bearer of the priests' washing-water. Neither reading cancels the other, and the number twelve invites a further thought the witnesses leave half-spoken — that as the oxen stood by threes to every quarter, so the twelve tribes surrounded and supported the worship of God. The great sea, borne aloft on beasts of brass, was both a marvel of proportion and a witness that the strength of the creature is fit only to serve, and never to receive, the honour due to God alone.
26:
The account of the sea closes with its thickness, its brim, and its vast capacity, and the witnesses reckon each in turn. John Gill glosses the thickness as "four fingers," a handbreadth of solid brass. Keil and Delitzsch confirm it — "its thickness was a handbreadth," as in the case of the pillars — and describe the rim as bent outward "like work of a goblet," ornamented with lily-blossom, so that the great basin flared at its lip into a ring of open flowers. The lily that crowned the pillars and bordered nothing above the gourds now shaped the very edge of the sea, flower answering to flower throughout the court.

On the capacity the witnesses meet another discrepancy with the Chronicles, and resolve it as they have resolved the others. This verse says the sea "contained two thousand baths," while the Chronicles say three thousand (2 Chronicles 4:5). Keil and Delitzsch judge the larger figure to have "arisen from the confusion of" the Hebrew letter for three with the letter for two, and note that, by calculation from the given dimensions, the vessel "could not exceed 2000 baths." Once more the grammatical-historical method traces a clash of numbers to a copyist's eye rather than to any fault in the record, and settles on the figure the measurements themselves require.

Keil closes with the vessel's purpose. Taking the place of the tabernacle laver, the molten sea was provided "for the priests to wash themselves" (2 Chronicles 4:6), holding water in readiness so that they might cleanse their hands and feet when they drew near the altar or entered the Holy Place, as the law had required at the laver (Exodus 30:18). The great flaring basin, a handbreadth thick and brimming with two thousand baths, was thus no ornament only but a working fountain of purification, its lily-rim and its huge store of water alike declaring that those who serve at God's altar must first be washed. The witnesses, having measured its brass and reckoned its baths, leave it standing full in the court — a sea of cleansing between the altar and the house, ready for every priest who would approach.
27:
The witnesses turn from the sea to the ten brazen bases, or stands, and set out their nature and purpose. John Gill calls them "seats, stands, or settles" for the ten lavers that would rest upon them; Adam Clarke more simply, "pedestals, for the ten lavers to rest on." Keil and Delitzsch give the name and dimensions: they were "stands or stools," so called from their office of supporting the basins used for washing the flesh of the sacrifices, "square chests cast in brass," each four cubits long, four broad, and three high.

Keil begins here the long and careful reconstruction of these elaborate vehicles that will run through the next several verses. The stands, he explains, were not plain boxes but framed structures: they had "surroundings," that is, panels forming their flat sides, set "between frames or borders which enclosed the sides and were connected together at the angles," and upon these panels, within their borders, were wrought figures of lions, oxen, and cherubim. The bare word "on this manner," with which the description opens, signals that the historian is about to give an unusually detailed account, and the grammatical-historical witnesses follow him detail by detail, sketching the panelled chest that formed the body of each base.

The verse yields no doctrine, and the witnesses press none, but the mere fact of ten such stands is itself significant. Where the tabernacle had a single laver, the temple was to have ten, each raised upon its own wrought and figured pedestal, so that the provision for washing was multiplied tenfold. The lions, oxen, and cherubim carved upon the panels — the strongest of beasts, the beast of sacrifice, and the guardians of the divine presence — turned even these serviceable water-stands into images of the created order gathered about the worship of God. The witnesses will spend their patience on wheels and undersetters and mouldings in the verses that follow, but they begin here with the plain frame: a square chest of brass, four cubits by four, adorned on every side, made to bear a laver of cleansing. From so utilitarian a thing, wrought so richly, the reader is meant to feel again how nothing about the house of God was left common or unadorned.
28:
This verse opens the detailed description of the bases' construction, and the witnesses labour to render its technical vocabulary. John Gill notes only that "the following was the form in which they were made," and leaves the particulars to be unfolded. Keil and Delitzsch supply them: the stands had "borders," which they render more exactly as panels, the flat sides of the chest, set "between frames or borders which enclosed the sides, and were connected together at the angles"; and upon these panels, within their enclosing borders, stood the figures the next verse will name. Above the panelled sides, Keil adds, "there was a base above," a pedestal set upon the chest to receive the basin, more fully described further on (v.31).

Keil is careful to correct a mistaken tradition here. The statement in Josephus, that each side "was divided into three compartments," he rejects as having "nothing to support it in the biblical text," and improbable in itself, since so many divisions "would have rendered the figures placed upon them insignificantly small." The panels were broad enough to bear the lions, oxen, and cherubim at a fitting size, not chopped into little squares. It is a small but telling instance of the method: even a respected ancient authority is measured against the text and the plain requirements of the design, and set aside where it fails them.

The verse, taken alone, is almost pure carpentry, and the witnesses attempt no edification from it; their whole care is to see the object rightly. Yet the very intricacy they trace — panels framed within borders, mortised at the corners, crowned with a pedestal — testifies to the workmanship lavished on things that would only hold water for the washing of sacrifices. The frames and panels the witnesses parse so minutely were, after all, the sides of a brazen cart. That the historian described them in such detail, and that the commentators follow him so faithfully, marks again the conviction running through the whole chapter: that in the house of God even the stand of a laver was a work of art, and that the record of it, technical as it is, was written to be understood and reverently reconstructed rather than skimmed. The figures upon the panels the next verse will describe.
29:
The witnesses come to the figures carved upon the panels of the bases and to the wreathed ornament about them. The panels bore "lions, oxen, and cherubims," and John Gill pauses over the third: since the cherubim are here distinguished from the lions and oxen, they must have been "figures of men, or else of eagles," those being the remaining faces of the composite cherubic form (compare the fourfold living creatures of Ezekiel 1:10). The three or four kinds of figure gathered on the base thus recall the faces of the cherubim themselves — lion, ox, man, and eagle — the whole order of living creatures wrought upon the sides of the stand.

Below the figured panels ran further ornament, "additions made of thin work," and here Keil and Delitzsch carry the description forward into the running-gear of the base. Each stand, they explain, had "four brazen wheels and brazen axles," and its four feet carried "shoulder-pieces" cast beneath the basin, with wreaths beyond them. The square chest, in other words, stood upon four feet fixed to axles and wheels of brass, "after the style of ordinary carriage wheels," raised high enough that the figured sides were left uncovered and the wheels ran clear below. The grammatical-historical reconstruction thus turns the base into something like a richly decorated wagon, its panels a gallery of living creatures, its underparts a set of brazen wheels.

The witnesses draw no explicit lesson, but the assemblage they describe is striking enough to speak for itself. Upon a wheeled carriage of brass were gathered the lion for strength, the ox for service and sacrifice, and the cherub for the presence of God, all bearing up a laver of cleansing. The creatures that in Ezekiel's vision upheld the throne and moved upon wheels are here, in Solomon's court, wrought in metal beneath the water of purification, as though the whole living creation were harnessed to the work of making the worshipper clean. Whether the artificer intended so full a symbolism the witnesses will not finally say; but they set the images down faithfully — lions, oxen, cherubim, and wheels — and let the reader feel the weight of a design in which even the movable stand of a laver gathered heaven and earth about the service of God.
30:
The witnesses describe the wheels and the supports of the base, and here they divide over a real question: were these wheels made to move, or only to bear? John Gill takes the plates of brass beneath the wheels to be flat pieces "on which the wheels stood, and not on the bare floor," and then presses a striking conclusion — that "these wheels seem only to serve as supporters, not to carry the laver from place to place, as is usually said." His reasons are practical: the wheels were not set on two sides like a chariot's but one at each square corner; and besides, once the lavers were placed upon the bases, "they were fixed in a certain place" (v.39), five on the right and five on the left of the house. On Gill's reading the wheels were ornamental feet, not a means of transport.

Keil and Delitzsch read them otherwise. The square chests, they hold, "stood upon axles with wheels of brass, after the style of ordinary carriage wheels" (v.33), "so that they could be driven or easily moved from one place to another." The chest did not rest directly on the axles but stood upon four feet fastened to them, which raised it clear of the wheels' rim, leaving the figured sides exposed and the wheels running free below. On Keil's reading the bases were genuinely mobile, wheeled vehicles that could be trundled where the water was needed.

The disagreement is not idle antiquarianism but a difference about what the artificer designed. Gill, struck by the fixed stations the lavers finally occupied, doubts they were ever meant to roll; Keil, struck by the plainly functional wheels, axles, and carriage-work, holds that they were. Neither can be simply refuted from the text, and the witnesses let the two readings stand side by side. What both agree upon is the ingenuity of the thing: a brazen chest set upon four wheels and undersetters, whether to be moved about the court or merely to be lifted clear of the floor, wrought with a mechanical cleverness that answered to its ornamental richness. Even in the plumbing of the temple, the witnesses observe, Solomon's craftsmen spent an art that later ages could only admire, so that the very stands of the lavers were at once figured galleries and feats of the founder's skill.
31:
This is confessedly the most obscure verse in the whole description, and the witnesses admit as much even as they attempt it. It describes the mouth or opening at the top of the base into which the laver was set. John Gill reconstructs it patiently: upon the lid of the base "rose up a lesser base, called the chapiter, which was circular, like a coronet," hollow within to receive the foot of the laver; it ran straight up half a cubit, then widened half a cubit more, and the circuit of this crown-like neck is "the mouth of the base" into which the laver's foot was set. Keil and Delitzsch render it that the mouth of the basin lay "within the crown and upwards with a cubit," the crown rounded like a pedestal, a cubit and a half wide, its panels square, not round.

Charles Ellicott is refreshingly candid about the whole. The verse, he says, is "most obscure, and in our version unintelligible." He sets down Keil's rendering in full, but is not satisfied even by it, objecting that "the rendering of the word 'mouth,' now for the laver, now for the chapiter, is arbitrary, and the whole is still obscure." He then offers his own tentative picture: since the circular stand was half a cubit deep, it looks as though the lower surface of the laver stood a cubit above the mouth; and if the laver were emptied by a cock near its bottom, this circular stand "may have received the drippings." The square top of the base and the round stand set upon it would leave open corners, and these, Ellicott suggests, may be the engraved "panels" the verse mentions.

The witnesses, then, do not pretend to a clarity the text does not yield. They rehearse the rival renderings, confess where the Hebrew defeats them, and venture reconstructions marked as guesses. This honesty is itself instructive. The grammatical-historical school does not smooth over an intractable verse or invent a false certainty; it lays the difficulty bare, weighs Keil against the Authorized Version, and admits that "the whole is still obscure." What can be seen through the fog they hold fast: a crowned, circular socket rising from the lid of each base, engraved about its square corners, into which the laver was fitted, a cubit and a half across. Beyond that they will not go, content to leave a hard verse honestly hard, and to let the outline of the thing stand where its every detail cannot.
32:
The witnesses return to firmer ground with the wheels and axletrees of the base. The four wheels stood "under the borders," and their axletrees were "joined to the base," each wheel "a cubit and half a cubit" — a cubit and a half — in height. John Gill is careful to say that these were not under the borders last mentioned but under those described earlier, keeping the parts of the intricate structure in their right relation. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying forward their running reconstruction, fit this verse into the whole: the wheels ran below the panelled sides of the chest, their axles fixed to the framework, so that the base rolled, or at least rested, upon four wheels a cubit and a half high.

The measurement matters to the grammatical-historical eye because it settles the proportions of the vehicle. A wheel a cubit and a half high, set under a chest three cubits tall, raised the figured panels well clear of the ground and gave the whole the aspect Keil has described, "the work of a chariot wheel" (v.33). Keil gathers this verse with those around it, noting how the axletrees were joined to the base and how the arched cover at the top (v.35) rose half a cubit above the sides, so that the reader may hold the entire structure in view at once — panelled chest, wheels below, crowned socket above.

No debate divides the witnesses here, and no lesson is pressed; the verse is a single dimension in a long specification. Yet the very exactness with which it is given, and with which the commentators receive it, belongs to the larger testimony of the chapter. The historian troubled to record the height of a wheel; the witnesses trouble to place that wheel correctly beneath the borders and to reckon its cubit and a half against the height of the chest. Behind such patience lies a shared conviction: that these were real objects, made to a real and recoverable design, and that the Spirit who moved the writer to preserve their measurements did not do so idly. The wheel of a cubit and a half, joined to its axle and set under the figured panels, takes its small place in the picture of a base at once ornamental and mechanical, wrought by Hiram's art for the service of the house of God.
33:
This verse likens the wheels of the bases to a familiar object and enumerates their parts, and the witnesses read it as confirming the mechanical ingenuity of the whole. "The work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel," and their several parts — "their axletrees, and their naves, and their felloes, and their spokes" — "were all molten," cast in brass. John Gill notes only that they were made "in the same form and fashion" as a chariot wheel, and cites the Targum's paraphrase to the same effect. Keil and Delitzsch take the comparison as decisive for their view of the bases: since the wheels were built exactly like ordinary carriage wheels, complete with hub, rim, and spokes, the stands were genuinely wheeled vehicles, made to be driven or moved (v.30).

The detail that every part was "molten" — hub, felloe, and spoke alike cast in metal — strikes the witnesses as a further mark of the founder's mastery. To cast a working wheel entire, with its axle and nave and radiating spokes, and to do so ten times over for the ten bases, was no small feat, and it belongs with the two colossal pillars and the great sea among the wonders of Hiram's brass-work. The wheels were not carved of wood and shod with metal but were themselves all of brass, run in the moulds of the plain of Jordan.

The verse gives the witnesses no occasion for controversy or doctrine, and they take none; its whole burden is the likeness to a chariot wheel and the casting of every part. Yet in that homely comparison lies something of the chapter's quiet wonder. The most sacred provision — water for the cleansing of the sacrifices offered to God — was carried upon wheels made just as a chariot's are made, the ordinary craft of the wheelwright taken up and wrought in costly brass for the house of the Lord. The witnesses, in noting so plain a thing, let the reader feel how the common skills of human making, the fashioning of a wheel with its nave and spokes, were consecrated to the service of the sanctuary, and how the God who filled Hiram with wisdom was content to be served by a craftsmanship that a passing traveller would have recognized from the roads of Israel.
34:
This short verse describes the four undersetters, or supports, at the corners of each base, and the witnesses read it as one more part of the elaborate frame. The undersetters were set "to the four corners of one base," and were "of the very base itself" — that is, cast in one piece with the chest, not fastened on afterward. John Gill glosses them briefly as "pillars," the corner-supports of the structure. Keil and Delitzsch, in their running account, take these to be the "shoulder-pieces" fixed at the four feet of the base, beneath the basin, cast together with the whole (v.30), so that the corners of the chest ran down into supporting brackets over the axles.

That the undersetters were "of the very base itself" is the point the witnesses mark. They were not separate props bolted to the corners but grew out of the casting, integral to the design, so that the base and its supports were one continuous work of brass. This unity of casting belongs to the same admiration the whole description has invited: the founder did not assemble his stands from many riveted pieces but ran them, so far as he could, as single moulded structures, the corner-brackets flowing from the same pour of metal as the panelled sides.

The verse yields nothing to dispute and little to expound, and the witnesses are brief accordingly. Yet even here the patient reconstruction serves a purpose, for the undersetters are what bore the weight of the whole — the loaded laver, the arched cover, the crowned socket — down onto the axles and wheels. The strength of the base lay in these corner-supports, and their being cast integral with the chest gave the structure its soundness. The witnesses, tracing part after part of a design most readers would hurry past, keep before us the completeness of the work: nothing improvised, nothing merely tacked on, but every member — panel, border, wheel, axle, and undersetter — belonging to a single conceived and executed whole. In so small a verse the chapter's steady lesson holds: that the work wrought for God's house was thorough to its least bracket, sound in the parts no eye would praise, and made of one piece from corner to corner.
35:
The witnesses come to the top of the base, the arched cover that received the laver, and they trace its form with the same care they have given the parts below. In the top of the base "was there a round compass of half a cubit high," and the ledges and borders of this crown were "of the same," cast with the rest. John Gill identifies this "round compass" with the chapiter already described: a circular neck that "rose up straight half a cubit, and widening upwards half a cubit more" (v.31), forming the socket for the laver. Keil and Delitzsch read the upper part of the square chest as "not flat, but rounded, i.e., arched," so that the covering rose half a cubit above the sides in a dome, and upon this dome stood the crown-like holders and panels into which the basin was set.

The design the witnesses recover is elegant. The base was a square chest below, but its lid swelled into an arched cover, and out of that arch rose a circular crown, half a cubit high, to hold the round laver. Thus the eye passed from the four-cornered panelled sides, through the rounded dome of the top, to the circular socket and the basin above — square resolving into round, the whole a studied composition of forms. The "ledges" and "borders" of this compass, Keil notes, were the holders that gripped the laver and the mouldings that framed the dome, all cast integral with the covering.

Here again the witnesses press no doctrine, and the verse offers none; it is the last structural member of a long specification. But with it the base stands complete in the mind, from its wheels to its crown, and the commentators' persistence is vindicated: what began as a tangle of obscure terms — borders, ledges, undersetters, compass — has been assembled, verse by verse, into a coherent and even beautiful object. The round compass at the top answers to the wheels at the bottom, curve echoing curve, and the arched cover gathers the whole into unity. The witnesses, having built the base up part by part, are ready now to describe the ornament graven upon it and the uniformity of the ten, and to set upon each its laver of cleansing. The engraving of this crown the next verse takes up.
36:
Upon the plates and borders of the base's crown Hiram graved yet more figures, and the witnesses attend to both the carving and a curious detail of the word used for it. The engraving was of "cherubims, lions, and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and additions round about." John Gill notes that the word here rendered "ledges" differs from the one used earlier and "literally signifies hands or handles"; and he reports the striking observation of Procopius of Gaza, that the bases had "in the upper part of them, forms of hands holding a circle like a crown." On this reading the socket that gripped the laver was fashioned as a pair of sculptured hands upholding a coronet — a conceit at once mechanical and ornamental.

Keil and Delitzsch gather this verse with the summary that follows, observing that Hiram "made the ten stools of one kind of casting, measure, and form," and set upon each a brazen basin holding forty baths, four cubits across (v.38). The engraving of cherubim, lions, and palm trees repeats, on the crown, the living figures already carved on the panels below (v.29), now joined by the palm, the emblem of fruitfulness and of the sanctuary, so that the whole base from foot to crown was a field of wrought figures.

The witnesses draw no formal doctrine, but the imagery they faithfully record gathers its own weight. Cherubim, lions, and palms — the guardians of God's presence, the strength of the beasts, and the flourishing of the righteous who are "planted in the house of the LORD" (Psalms 92:13) — were graven upon the very stands that bore the water of cleansing, and, if Procopius saw rightly, hands of brass reached up to hold the laver like a crown. Even here the phrase "according to the proportion of every one" catches the witnesses' notice: each figure was carved to its fitting size, the whole kept in measure, nothing crowded or misshapen.
