The care that ran through pillar and sea runs on into the least ledge of the base, so that the reader is left once more with the impression the chapter everywhere presses — a house of God in which strength, life, and the presence of heaven were wrought into every serviceable thing, and even the handles of a laver-stand were made to bear the images of glory.
37:
This verse rounds off the account of the bases by declaring their uniformity: "after this manner he made the ten bases: all of them had one casting, one measure, and one size." John Gill notes simply that "this was the form and fashion of them as above described," the single pattern to which all ten conformed. Keil and Delitzsch underline the threefold sameness — "one kind of casting, measure, and form" — and pass on to the ten basins that would rest upon them, each holding forty baths (v.38).

The point the witnesses draw from the verse is the deliberate identity of the ten. There was no variation from stand to stand, no one made larger or more richly than another; all were run from one mould, to one measure, in one form, so that the ten bases stood in the court as ten exact replicas, five on either side of the house. The elaborate design the previous verses have reconstructed — panelled chest, wheels and axles, undersetters, arched cover, crowned socket, and graven figures of cherubim, lions, oxen, and palms — was repeated without difference ten times over. What Hiram had conceived once, he cast ten times alike.

Here the grammatical-historical patience over detail yields a quiet aesthetic and even theological point, though the witnesses leave it mostly unspoken. Uniformity on this scale was itself a mark of mastery: to cast ten intricate structures identical in every measure, and to fill each with a basin of the same forty baths, spoke of a designer in full command of his art. And the repeated sameness suited the house of God, where the ten stands ranged in matching order lent the court the same disciplined symmetry that governed the windows of the palace and the courses of its stone. The one casting, one measure, one size of the bases answered to the single mind behind the whole work, in which nothing was left to caprice and every part answered to every other. Having finished the stands, the witnesses turn with the historian to the lavers they were made to bear, and to the placing of the whole about the house.
38:
Upon the ten bases were set the ten lavers, and here the witnesses pass from the stands to the basins they bore, and from architecture toward type. Each laver held forty baths and was four cubits across, one to every base. John Gill marks at once the increase upon the tabernacle: "there was but one in the tabernacle of Moses," but here were ten, "enough provided for the purpose for the priests to wash their burnt offerings in." Keil and Delitzsch add the placing — five on the right side of the house and five on the left — and note from the Chronicles that the basins served "for washing... the work of the burnt-offering" (2 Chronicles 4:6), while the great sea was reserved for the priests themselves. Adam Clarke confirms the division of use: the ten lavers were "to wash the victims that were to be offered as a burnt-offering... but the brazen sea was for the priests to wash in."

It is Gill, with the whole Puritan tradition behind him, who lifts the lavers into figure. These ten basins of cleansing were, he says, "typical of the large provision made in the blood of Christ for the cleansing of his people, whose works, services, and sacrifices, as well as persons and garments, need continual washing in that blood." Matthew Henry, surveying the same vessels, presses the point home: "spiritual priests and spiritual sacrifices must be washed in the laver of Christ's blood and of regeneration. We must wash often, for we daily contract pollution." The multiplication of lavers, ten where once there was one, becomes for the witnesses a parable of abundance — a cleansing so ample that, as Henry says, "if we have our lot for ever among the unclean it will be our own fault."

Here the grammatical-historical care for use and the Puritan eye for type meet without strain. Keil and Clarke distinguish exactly what was washed in the lavers and what in the sea; Gill and Henry read in the very number and capacity of the basins the largeness of gospel grace. Both are looking at ten brazen lavers of forty baths apiece, ranged five and five about the house. The witnesses agree that these were working vessels for the washing of sacrifice, and agree too that they preached, in brass and water, the necessity and the plenty of a deeper cleansing — the blood that washes the worshipper, and the sacrifice, and the very service, clean.
39:
This verse sets the lavers and the sea in their places about the house, and the witnesses attend to the geography of the court. Five bases stood "on the right side of the house" and five "on the left," and the sea "on the right side eastward, over against the south." John Gill takes the right and left to mark the south and north sides, where the lavers were ranged "for the use" of the priests in the courts; and he notes the small complication that, since the entrance lay to the east, a man going in would have the north on his right and the south on his left, which shapes how the directions are read. Keil and Delitzsch settle it plainly: "the right side is the south side, and the left the north side," so that the stands were placed not on either side of the altar but on either side of the temple-hall, while the brazen sea stood farther forward, "between the hall and the altar," toward the south-east.

Charles Ellicott agrees, placing the sea "on the south-eastern side of the Temple, on one side of the great altar," with "the ten smaller lavers ranged five on each side." The witnesses thus reconstruct the furnished court: the two pillars before the porch, the great sea to the south-east, and the ten wheeled lavers drawn up five and five along the flanks of the house, a whole apparatus of cleansing arranged in ordered symmetry about the place of sacrifice.

No dispute divides them here beyond the momentary puzzle of right and left, and the witnesses draw no explicit doctrine; the verse is a plan of the court. Yet the arrangement itself carries the impression the chapter has built throughout. Everything stood in its appointed place, balanced side against side, the lavers matched five to five, the sea set where the priest passing from the altar to the house would find water ready. The same love of order that ranged the windows of the palace and cast the ten bases to one measure now disposed the vessels of the court, so that the worshipper's eye met symmetry wherever it turned. The witnesses, tracing south and north, right and left, leave the temple court fully furnished in the mind — a place where the provision for cleansing was not only ample but ordered, ranged about the house of God with the deliberate proportion that marked the whole of Solomon's work.
40:
With the great pieces described, the historian records that Hiram completed the smaller vessels and so "made an end of doing all the work." The verse names "the lavers, and the shovels, and the basins," and the witnesses clarify both the objects and a notable silence. John Gill explains that these "lavers" are not the ten already described but what are elsewhere called pots, used "to put the ashes of the altar into"; the shovels were "a sort of besoms to sweep them off," and the basins "to receive the blood of the sacrifices, and sprinkle it." Charles Ellicott confirms that the word should be "pots," as in the later summary (v.45), and that the verse "describes the completion of Hiram's work by the making of the smaller vessels."

Both Gill and Ellicott mark what is missing: no mention is made here of the brazen altar. Ellicott finds it "curious," and weighs the suggestion that the old altar reared by David (2 Samuel 24:25) was retained; but since the Chronicles and Josephus expressly record that Hiram made a brazen altar thirty feet square and fifteen high (2 Chronicles 4:1), he concludes that "the absence of all mention of it here is simply an omission in the record." The grammatical-historical witnesses thus note the gap honestly and fill it from the parallel accounts, rather than pretending the altar was never made.

It is Adam Clarke who voices the wonder that the summary verse invites. "It is truly surprising," he writes, "that in so short a time one artist could design and execute works of such magnitude, taste, and variety, however numerous his assistants might be." The mere building of the house, he adds, "was a matter of little difficulty in comparison of these internal works" — the pillars, the sea, the ten wheeled bases, and now the multitude of pots, shovels, and basins. The witnesses close the account of the brass with Clarke's admiration and with the historian's own note of completion: Hiram had made an end of all the work he made for king Solomon for the house of the Lord. What one Tyrian craftsman, filled with wisdom, had wrought in the clay-ground of the Jordan now stood finished and furnished in the courts of God.
41:
The witnesses reach the summary catalogue in which the historian numbers over again the chief works of brass, and they read it as an inventory drawn up to fix the record. This verse recounts "the two pillars, and the two bowls of the chapiters that were on the top of the two pillars; and the two networks, to cover the two bowls of the chapiters." Keil and Delitzsch, who guide the reader through the whole list, refer each item back to its fuller description — the pillars and their capitals to the earlier verses (vv.15-22) — and note in passing a small textual slip: the phrase "upon the face of the pillars" in the next verse "is probably a mistake for 'upon the two pillars,'" since the capitals could not properly be said to sit upon the surface of the columns.

The "bowls" here are the rounded globes of the capitals, the swelling form already called the "belly" (v.20), and the "networks" the latticed festoons that covered them. The summary thus gathers into a single sentence the pillars, their crowning bowls, and the net-work upon the bowls, recapitulating the elaborate ornament the earlier verses unfolded piece by piece. The witnesses follow the recapitulation faithfully, matching each term to what has gone before, so that the reader may see the parts once more as a numbered whole.

No debate or doctrine attaches to so plain a tally, and the witnesses attempt none; yet the very existence of the summary is worth their notice. The historian was not content to describe the great works once; he counted them over at the end, as a steward numbers what has been made and delivered — two pillars, two bowls, two networks — that nothing might be forgotten or lost from the account. This careful reckoning belongs to the same spirit that measured every cubit and named every ornament: a determination that the furnishing of God's house should stand fully recorded. The witnesses, in tracing the catalogue against the detailed description, honour that determination, and read the repetition not as redundancy but as the deliberate sealing of the inventory. The pomegranates and the remaining vessels the following verses will number in their turn.
42:
The catalogue continues with the pomegranates and the bases, and the witnesses again match each entry to its earlier account while noting the arithmetic. This verse numbers "four hundred pomegranates for the two networks, even two rows of pomegranates for one network," and passes on to "the ten bases, and ten lavers on the bases." Keil and Delitzsch refer the stands and their basins back to the full description (vv.27-37), and confirm the total of four hundred pomegranates — two hundred to each pillar — which the summary here makes explicit and which resolves the omission noted earlier in the text (v.20).

Keil pauses over a textual matter that touches the honesty of transmission. The Greek translators, he observes, added at this point an interpolation about "forty-eight pillars of the king's house and the house of God," which he judges "apocryphal," betrayed by its "marvellous combination of the king's house and the house of God," though Thenius had taken it for a genuine notice. The grammatical-historical witness thus sifts the received text from later accretion, keeping the record clean of a gloss that does not belong. He also fixes the metal: these vessels were of "polished brass," the material named as the accusative governed by the verb.

The summary, once more, yields no controversy of doctrine and no occasion for the pulpit, and the witnesses press neither; their work is the patient checking of the inventory. Four hundred pomegranates, ten bases, ten lavers — each is set down and squared with what went before, the round number of the fruit at last completed, the stands and basins gathered into the tally. Behind the dry enumeration lies the same concern that has governed the whole chapter, that the works of the house be truly and fully counted; and the witnesses' care to distinguish the authentic text from the Greek interpolation shows that this concern reaches even to the wording of the account. That the pomegranates, having been left with a broken count earlier (v.20), are here rounded out to their full four hundred is itself a small vindication of the historian's method: what one verse leaves incomplete, another supplies, so that the whole record, taken together, yields a coherent tally. The reckoning of the sea and the smaller vessels follows in the verses that close the list.
43:
This verse continues the closing inventory of the brass-work, naming "the ten bases, and ten lavers on the bases." It stands almost bare of comment in the witnesses, and the one entry attached to it in the sources belongs, in fact, to another book and another story altogether — a stray note about Samuel and the men of Israel that has plainly wandered in by a copyist's or compiler's error, and has nothing to do with Solomon's temple. Setting that intrusion aside, the verse must be read, as Keil and Delitzsch read it, simply as one line of the summary catalogue, gathering the ten stands and their ten basins into the tally of all that Hiram made (vv.27-37).

The repetition of the bases and lavers here, after their full description earlier and their mention in the previous verse, is itself the point worth marking. The historian counts them over deliberately, as part of the ordered reckoning that runs through the whole of this closing section: two pillars, their bowls and networks, four hundred pomegranates, and now the ten bases with the ten lavers upon them, each item numbered and set in its place. The ten stands, wrought to one casting and one measure, and the ten basins of forty baths apiece, ranged five and five about the house (v.39), are here formally entered into the inventory of the temple's furniture.

No debate divides the witnesses over so plain a notice, and no doctrine is pressed from it; the verse is a single line in a steward's account. Yet even here the chapter's steady character holds. What was made for God's house was not only wrought with art but carefully told and re-told, that the record might be complete and nothing slip from memory. The ten bases and their lavers, the movable and figured vehicles of cleansing that the witnesses reconstructed at such length, are gathered up in this brief line and laid, as it were, upon the finished ledger of the sanctuary. The narrative moves next to the great sea and the twelve oxen, and then to the multitude of smaller vessels, as the summary draws toward its close and the vast weight of unnumbered brass is at last confessed.
44:
The catalogue turns to the greatest vessel of all, "one sea, and twelve oxen under the sea," and the witnesses, having described these fully before (vv.23-26), use the summary chiefly to record where and how such enormous castings were made. Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the notice that follows in the text: "in the plain of Jordan did the king cast them... in the clay ground between Succoth and Zarthan." The ground there, they explain, was "marly throughout," and the forms were made "by stamping together the clayey soil," so that the molten brass could be run into moulds shaped in the earth itself. Keil fixes the geography with care: Succoth lay east of the Jordan, on the south side of the Jabbok, and Zarthan on the western side, probably at Kurn Sartabeh; and the casting must have been done on the western bank, since the eastern side "has scarcely any level ground at all."

The single sea and its twelve supporting oxen head this portion of the list, and the summary sets them among the works whose sheer mass the next verse will confess was beyond weighing. The witnesses note how the historian gathers the largest and smallest together — the vast sea, the twelve oxen, and then the pots, shovels, and basins — all "of bright brass," the whole made by Hiram for king Solomon for the house of the Lord.

The verse yields no controversy, but the detail of the casting-place quietly enlarges the reader's sense of the undertaking. These were not vessels bought or borrowed but founded on the spot, in the clay flats of the Jordan valley, where the very soil was fit for moulds and the ground broad enough to bear the work. The witnesses, tracing Succoth and Zarthan on the map and testing which bank could hold the foundry, keep before us the labour behind the splendour: the great sea that stood so serenely in the temple court had first been poured, glowing, into forms pressed in Jordan mud, miles from Jerusalem. That so colossal a thing could be cast at all, and cast in the wilderness of the river-plain, belongs to the wonder the witnesses have felt throughout at Hiram's mastery of the brass.
46:
This verse names the place of casting, and the witnesses gather about the clay-ground of the Jordan valley to explain why the work was done there and not at Jerusalem. John Gill gives the reason plainly: the ground was "thick," as the word signifies, "and stiff and close," and so "fit for such a purpose as casting brass"; for of such clay were the furnaces and moulds of the founder made. The great pieces — the two pillars, the sea, the ten lavers — required moulds shaped in the ground, "and so the melted brass was poured into them, which gave it its different forms." Adam Clarke agrees that Hiram "found that particular kind of clay that was proper for his purpose," and reports the conjecture, with Calmet, that the foundry stood near Bethshan.

Charles Ellicott maps the two towns between which the casting was done. Succoth lay "on the east side of Jordan, in the territory of Gad," the place of Jacob's halt on his way from Padan-aram (Genesis 33:17) and of the insult offered to Gideon and his revenge (Judges 8:5); Zarthan, or Zaretan, lay "on the western side, in the territory of Manasseh, not far from Bethshan," and near the crossing where the Jordan's waters had stood up for Israel (Joshua 3:16). The grammatical-historical witnesses thus set the foundry in a landscape thick with the memories of the nation's past.

It is Matthew Henry who supplies the homely reason of decorum. The casting was done in the plain of Jordan "because the ground there was stiff and clayey, fit to make moulds of," and also, he adds, because "Solomon would not have this dirty smoky work done in or near Jerusalem." The holy city was not to be fouled with the smoke and grime of the foundry; the rough labour was carried off to the river-plain, and only the finished, gleaming vessels brought up to the house of God. The witnesses draw no heavy doctrine from the clay-ground, but the picture they leave is eloquent enough: the splendour that adorned the sanctuary was born in the mud and smoke of a distant valley, wrought where the ground was fit for the work, and then carried clean into the courts of the Lord. What was to stand in glory before God was first cast, unseen, in the stiff clay between Succoth and Zarthan.
47:
The historian confesses that the vessels were so many that Solomon left them unweighed, and the witnesses read the notice as a mark at once of abundance and of the king's care. John Gill takes "left" in the sense of laid up: Solomon "brought them from whence they were cast, and put them in the sanctuary without weighing them," for they were "so large... and so numerous." The weight of the brass was simply "not found out," never reckoned. Keil and Delitzsch guard the sense of the verb — not that he set them down, but that "he let them lie, i.e., unweighed" — and trace the source of so much metal: this brass, according to the Chronicles, David had taken "from the cities of Hadadezer," adding the brass presented by Toi (1 Chronicles 18:8).

Charles Ellicott follows the same thread, naming the cities Tibhath and Chun of the territory of Zobah from which David drew the store, and observing that these places may have grown rich in brass from "very ancient copper-mines, once worked by the Egyptians, in the Sinaitic peninsula," with a glance at the mining described in Job (Job 28:1-11). The grammatical-historical witnesses thus follow the metal back to its origin in war-spoil and mine, filling out the history behind the temple's brass.

It is Matthew Henry who draws the double lesson from the unweighed vessels. That the brass went unnumbered and unweighed argues both the plenty of the material and the honesty of the workmen: "so honest were the workmen, and such great plenty of brass they had, that there was no danger of wanting." And the surplus was no accident but foresight — "we must ascribe it to Solomon's care that he provided so much, not to his carelessness that he kept no account of it," for having wherewithal to do so, "he provided for posterity." The witnesses agree in refusing to read the missing tally as negligence. The weight was left unknown not because the king was careless but because the store was so vast and the workmen so trustworthy that reckoning it was needless; and in providing beyond all present need, Solomon laid up brass against the future wants of the house, an image of a bounty so great that it could not be measured.
48:
From the brass of the court the historian turns to the gold of the Holy Place, and the witnesses mark both the material and the meaning of the sacred vessels. Keil and Delitzsch note that the golden furniture is set out in two classes made "of fine gold," with a third of gold of lesser purity, and identify each piece — "the golden altar, and the golden table on which was the shew-bread, and the candlesticks... of costly gold." John Gill observes that all these were newly made, "even all that were in the tabernacle of Moses... excepting the ark, mercy seat, and cherubim," which alone were carried over from the old sanctuary into the new.

Charles Ellicott unfolds the symbolism of the altar of gold, which is the altar of incense. Upon it incense was burned morning and evening, its horns touched with the blood of the sin-offering and purified on the Day of Atonement, so that the offering of incense "pre-supposed sacrifice already offered, and atonement made for sin." To Israel it "symbolised the offering of an acceptable worship by man, as restored to the love and communion of God" (Psalms 141:2); to us it figures "the intercession of the One Mediator, offered for us in the Most Holy Place of heaven" (Hebrews 9:11-12). The shew-bread, "bread of the presence," he reads as a eucharistic offering of God's own gift of bread, a first-fruits confessing that all the sustenance of life comes from him.

It is Matthew Henry who gathers the gold-work into a gospel. All within was gold, and all made new, "the golden altar, table, and candlestick, with all their appurtenances." The altar of incense was still but one, "for Christ and his intercession are so"; yet the tables and candlesticks were multiplied to ten (2 Chronicles 4:8), "intimating the much greater plenty both of spiritual food and heavenly light which the gospel blesses us with than the law of Moses did or could afford." Here the witnesses converge: the grammatical-historical readers identify the vessels and expound their ancient use and their fulfilment in Christ; the Puritan reads their gold and their number as the promise of gospel abundance. The one golden altar preaches the one Mediator; the many tables and lamps, the fullness of food and light that flows from him.
49:
The witnesses number the golden candlesticks and their furniture and dwell on the increase from the tabernacle's one to the temple's ten. The candlesticks were "of pure gold, five on the right side, and five on the left, before the oracle," with their flowers, lamps, and tongs of gold. John Gill marks the multiplication: where Moses had one candlestick, here were ten, which "may denote the greater degree of spiritual light in the church of Christ under the Gospel dispensation than under the law." Charles Ellicott weighs whether these ten superseded or supplemented the ancient seven-branched lamp of the tabernacle (Exodus 25:31), and judges the latter "far more probable," both because it seems unlikely the old sacred candlestick was disused, and because the second temple had but one such lamp, the very one carried in the Roman triumph and carved on the Arch of Titus.

Ellicott expounds the candlestick's meaning as he had the altar's. Placed in the Holy Place opposite the table of shew-bread and fed with the sacred oil, it "appears to have symbolised the gift of light to the world, as the shew-bread the gift of life and sustenance, flowing from the presence of God." He identifies the appurtenances from the description of the tabernacle — the "flowers" the ornaments of stem and branch, the "lamps" the seven lights, the "tongs" for trimming — keeping the temple's furniture in continuity with the older sanctuary.

The witnesses draw no dispute from the verse beyond Ellicott's careful question of supersession, and their reading converges on light. The gold candlesticks, five and five before the inner shrine, poured their flame across the Holy Place toward the table of the bread of the presence, so that life and light stood answering each other before the veil. Gill and Ellicott alike hear in the tenfold lamp a promise: that the light which shone dimly from one candlestick under the law would blaze more fully under the gospel, the church filled with a brightness the tabernacle only foreshadowed. Fed with pure oil and set in pure gold, ranged in the same ordered symmetry that governed the whole house, the candlesticks lit the Holy Place as a figure of the greater illumination to come, when the true Light would give light to all who draw near to God.
50:
The catalogue of the golden furniture ends with the smaller vessels and the very hinges of the doors, and the witnesses note how the costliness reached even to the least things. The list runs through "the bowls, and the snuffers, and the basins, and the spoons, and the censers of pure gold; and the hinges of gold." John Gill glosses the bowls as vessels "to keep the oil in" for the lamps. Charles Ellicott corrects several of the renderings from the older description of the tabernacle furniture: the "snuffers" are properly knives or trimming-tools, the word coming from a root meaning "to prune"; the "spoons" are rather incense-pans, the name signifying "something hollow," said elsewhere to have been "full of incense" (Numbers 7:86); and the "censers" are better rendered snuff-dishes or ash-pans, as in the account of the lampstand (Exodus 25:38).

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the hinges, which were "also of gold, possibly of inferior quality" — the third class of gold noted in this section. These were "either the hinges of the doors, or more probably the sockets, in which the pegs of the doors turned," provided for the doors of the inner house, the Most Holy Place, and for the doors of the temple itself. Even the pivots on which the doors swung were of gold.

It is Matthew Henry who catches the point of so exhaustive a list. "Even the hinges of the door were of gold," he observes, "that every thing might be alike magnificent, and bespeak Solomon's generosity." He records the further suggestion that all was made thus splendid "to keep the people from idolatry, for none of the idol-temples were so rich and fine as this" — though, he adds soberly, "how little the expedient availed the event showed." The witnesses, correcting the names of pans and knives and tracing the gold down to the door-sockets, leave the reader with a single strong impression: that in the house of God nothing was too small to be made precious. From the bowls of lamp-oil to the ash-pans to the hidden pivots of the doors, all was gold, so that the magnificence was not for show alone but ran through the whole, complete even in the parts that no worshipper would ever see or handle.
51:
The chapter closes with the finishing of the work and the bringing in of David's dedicated treasures, and the witnesses read the double notice as a fitting seal upon the whole. John Gill observes that "all was completely finished in the space of seven years," the term of the temple's building; the work that Solomon "ordered to be made... for the ornament of it, or for the use and service of it" was now done. Then Solomon "brought in the things which David his father had dedicated" — the silver, the gold, and the vessels — and "put them among the treasures of the house of the LORD."

Adam Clarke draws from this a conclusion about the temple's making. It appears, he argues, that Solomon did not spend his father's dedicated gold and silver on the building itself, "for after the house was finished, with all its utensils and ornaments," it is only now that he brings in David's store and lays it up. Solomon had rather employed years in his own preparation, and "the temple was truly Solomon's, as he had provided all its materials, and borne every expense," while David's treasure was reserved and "placed among the treasures of the house." Charles Ellicott fills out the history of that treasure, gathered "out of the spoils of many victories" and offered in the spirit of David's own words, "of thine own have we given thee" (1 Chronicles 29:14).

It is Matthew Henry who draws the lesson of the dedicated things brought in. What was not spent in the building was "laid up in the treasury, for repairs, exigencies, and the constant charge of the temple-service"; and he presses the duty of honouring a parent's devotion: "what the parents have dedicated to God the children ought by no means to alienate or recall," but rather to devote cheerfully what was set apart for holy uses. Above all, Henry lifts the finished house toward its antitype: "Christ is now the Temple and the Builder; the Altar and the Sacrifice; the Light of our souls, and the Bread of life," able to supply all the wants of all who come to him. So the witnesses end where the chapter has tended throughout. The last brass is cast, the last gold laid up, the father's gifts brought in and treasured; the house stands finished and furnished, a completed work of many hands and many years — and, for the Christian reader, a shadow of the greater house whose builder and whose glory is Christ himself.
